The product parses the output of 'sshd -T' for the key that the system uses for communication and ensures that key is appropriately protected. The other keys in that location may not have the correct permissions applied. https://github.com/simp/pupmod-simp-ssh/issues/150 has been created to address this issue.

This check will fail if there is a commented out line that looks like "#PrintLastLog = <value>" even if the correct value of "PrintLastLog = true" exists in the /etc/ssh/sshd_config file.

There is no reliable way to remove extraneous umask commands without causing other errors. However, the umask that the product sets should take precedence over any others in the file since it will be located at the bottom.

The product will set the mode of the files referenced in this rule to "700" and "600" rather then "750" and "640". This will lock down the files and keep them secure so only admins and ISSMs can access them.

The product cannot dictate how a user's PKI infrastructure should look like. It is quite possible that forcing a user to use DOD root certificates and CAs as well as certificates signed by said CAs would interfere with the current PKI infrastructure of the user's site.
The product cannot make the determination of what username an organization should use for their grub superuser.
The product cannot make the determination of what username an organization should use for their grub superuser.

The cron command to run aide is configured, but it will not be configured to mail the logs to any particular email because this is highly system-specific. If a user wishes to configure this they can add the following hieradata: `aide::cron_command: /usr/sbin/aide --check |/bin/mail -s "$HOSTNAME - Daily AIDE integrity check run" root@example_server_name.mil`
The product will not walk the entire file system for any rule to prevent issues with large disks and file shares.
The product will not walk the entire file system for any rule to prevent issues with large disks and file shares.
The product cannot determine or enforce who the PKI user is on the system as it will vary from organization to organization.
The product can't determine which accounts are temporary to set them to expire.
The product can't determine the appropriate size to allocate for a weeks worth of audit logs this will vary from organization to organization.
The product can't determine the appropriate chrony server to sync to since this will vary from organization to organization.
Iptables will be the backend being used with the product.
Every rule required by the STIG that checks specific files will have xattrs option set, however, this is more of a manual audit check rather than a specific rule.
Every rule required by the STIG that checks specific files will have acl option set, however, this is more of a manual audit check rather than a specific rule.
